body_txt: In the past, when a person moved to another country, it was common for that person to change their name to one from the new country's dominant culture. The reasons for doing this were numerous; they could want to "blend in" better, they may want to avoid discrimination, or they may want to symbolize a complete break from their past (especially if they're on the run for something they did back in the old country). At other times, the name change can be forced on them by an overworked or racist immigration official (although in reality this is somewhat of an urban legend, or at least the immigration officials who did this have been Mis-blamed ; when this did happen, it was usually the result of officials copying misspelled names from the shipping companies' passenger manifests). Although less common today, this was once very much Truth in Television , and still happens on occasion. It's frequently featured in fiction that takes place in those time periods. It's also still common for people temporarily living in another country to use a local name for ease of socialization, without legally changing their old name. The most common types of name changes: Sometimes the name will be changed more than once, so the name "Rothstein" might get changed to "Roth", and then "Roth" gets changed to "Ross". The name change doesn't necessarily have to happen at the time of arrival; it might happen even if a family's been in a country for generations, if circumstances occur such as a wave of political Patriotic Fervor , or if the country they live in goes to war with the country their last name sounds like it's from (such as having a German-sounding last name in America during WWI). When a character translates their name to use as an alias, it's This Is My Name on Foreign . Compare Dub Name Change , where the character's name is changed due to Real Life choices, not In-Universe ones.
